question: Which geographical feature of the Americas most significantly hindered the spread of agricultural innovations compared to Eurasia's east-west orientation?
options: Lack of navigable rivers
The north-south orientation and diverse climate zones
The presence of the Appalachian Mountains
Extensive desert regions in Central America
explanation: Strayer emphasizes that the north-south axis of the Americas forced crops to adapt to vastly different climate and vegetation zones, slowing their diffusion. (Strayer, Ch. 6: Continental Comparisons)

question: How did the 'cities without states' in the Niger River Valley maintain social order without a centralized government?
options: Through a powerful religious priesthood
Through specialized economic groups organized into occupational castes
By relying on a standing army of mercenaries
Through strict hereditary monarchy
explanation: Order was maintained through horizontal organization, where specialized groups like blacksmiths and weavers functioned independently but cooperatively. (Strayer, Ch. 6: Africa - Niger River Valley)

question:
_"I am the first and greatest of the kings of the Axumites... I have brought under my subjection the peoples who border on my kingdom... I have commanded them to pay tribute, and they have obeyed."_
— Inscription on a stone throne at Adulis (c. 2nd-3rd century C.E.)

What does this text suggest about the nature of the early Axumite state?
options: It was a peaceful democracy focused on internal trade
It was an expansionist military power that demanded tribute from conquered neighbors
It was a small, isolated kingdom with no foreign contact
It was primarily a religious community with no military force
explanation: The inscription explicitly details the king's conquests and the subjection of neighboring peoples to pay tribute, indicating a centralized military state. (Strayer, Ch. 6, Source 6.1)

question:
_"I am the first and greatest of the kings of the Axumites... I have brought under my subjection the peoples who border on my kingdom... I have commanded them to pay tribute, and they have obeyed."_
— Inscription on a stone throne at Adulis (c. 2nd-3rd century C.E.)

Based on this text, what was a key method used by Axumite kings to demonstrate their authority?
options: Building public libraries for the common people
Recording their military triumphs on permanent stone monuments
Distributing gold equally among all citizens
Abolishing the system of tribute
explanation: The existence of the stone throne inscription itself is evidence that Axumite rulers used monumental records to legitimize and display their power. (Strayer, Ch. 6, Source 6.1)

question:
_"When the king of that country [Axum] wants to trade for gold, he takes with him a large quantity of cattle and salt and iron... They [the locals] bring gold in small nuggets and place one or two on the meat or salt... and then they retire."_
— Cosmas Indicopleustes, The Christian Topography (6th century C.E.)

This description of "silent trade" most directly illustrates Axum's role in which network?
options: The Silk Road land routes through Central Asia
The Indian Ocean trade network connecting Africa to the Mediterranean
The Trans-Atlantic slave trade
The North Sea fishing industry
explanation: Cosmas was a Greek merchant-monk whose account details Axum's active participation in the maritime and inland trade routes connecting East Africa with the wider world. (Strayer, Ch. 6, Source 6.4)

question: Which intellectual achievement was unique to the Maya among all pre-Columbian American civilizations?
options: The domestication of the llama
The construction of large earthen mounds
A fully developed and complex writing system
The use of iron tools in agriculture
explanation: The Maya developed the most sophisticated and complete writing system in the Americas, capable of recording historical events and complex astronomical data. (Strayer, Ch. 6: The Americas - The Maya)

question: What was the primary religious significance of the Chavín de Huántar temple complex in the Andes?
options: It was a military fortress for the Inca Empire
It served as a pan-regional pilgrimage site that unified the region through a shared cult
It was the site of the world's first Christian church
It was a secular school for astronomical study
explanation: Chavín de Huántar was a central religious hub where pilgrims from the coast and highlands gathered, spreading a common artistic and religious style. (Strayer, Ch. 6: The Americas - Chavín)

question: What was a major consequence of the Bantu migrations for the indigenous Paleolithic peoples of sub-Saharan Africa?
options: They were invited to lead the new Bantu kingdoms
They were largely displaced or absorbed by the agricultural and iron-working Bantu societies
They successfully resisted the migrations using iron weapons
They adopted Christianity from the Bantu people
explanation: The Bantu's advantages in agriculture and iron technology allowed them to outnumber and eventually absorb or push out the hunter-gatherer groups they encountered. (Strayer, Ch. 6: Africa - Bantu Africa)

question:
_"The king was then moved by the words of the boy... and he received the grace of baptism, and he commanded that the people should also be taught the faith of Christ."_
— Rufinus, On the Evangelization of Abyssinia (late 4th century C.E.)

This account describes the conversion of which African kingdom's royal court?
options: Meroë
The Niger River Valley
Axum
Carthage
explanation: Rufinus's account describes how the Christian faith was introduced to the Axumite court, leading to King Ezana's conversion. (Strayer, Ch. 6, Source 6.3)

question: How did the political structure of the Maya differ from that of the city of Teotihuacán?
options: The Maya were a unified empire, while Teotihuacán was a collection of city-states
The Maya were organized into competing city-states, while Teotihuacán was a single, massive planned metropolis
Neither society had any form of political organization
The Maya had no cities, while Teotihuacán was the only city in Mesoamerica
explanation: Maya civilization was characterized by fragmented and warring city-states (similar to Greece), whereas Teotihuacán was a highly centralized and planned urban power. (Strayer, Ch. 6: The Americas)
